speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Borovick is being interviewed. Mr. Borovick, you-- you-- when you contacted your cousin and you were ready to go to Soviet Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to-- I-- I-- in order to go to-- to go to-- to the border of Poland and Russia, I had to go through that little town, [INAUDIBLE]. So I went to go to [INAUDIBLE] and-- and-- and he joined me. And then a few other boys. And we went together on the road. We were on the road about a week or so and when-- until we came to a little town not far from the border. Then the German-- the Germans caught us.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- this was right after the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it was you, your cousin-- was your brother there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a group of people. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your-- not-- not your brother. You and your cousin and a group of others. Tell us about how you were captured by the Germans. How were you traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the-- on the road. I forgot the name of the town where we were, not far from-- not-- not too far from the border. So all of a sudden-- all of a sudden, a-- a group of civil people-- they were parachutists-- is it in English, right? Parachutists?

speaker: interviewer
text: Parachutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parachutists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they said, are you-- are you Jewish? And they said something in German then about us. And then they say-- they say, you go home now. They were Germans. We went home. We went to-- to the-- there were-- there were leaflets. The airplanes spread leaflets that wherever-- wherever a-- a person is has to go to his home. They wanted-- they wanted to have us in one place in order to send that-- to send the people in the-- to the-- to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told you to go, but they didn't take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went. They didn't take-- didn't [? do ?] anything. We just went to-- went to-- they let us go. So I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that-- these two weeks that we were on the road, two or-- or maybe more, a little. During that time, my-- my-- during that year, when I was-- told my-- told my mother that I'm running, I said to my brother-in-law, I-- do you want to join me? I have to-- I have to go to the-- I'm going to the border to search Russia. He said, oh yes. Yes, let's go. And then he said, oh no. I can't. I could not leave your-- my wife, your sister, and my little-- my little son. He had a son, Abrasha. It was a poem in my book, 'Abrasha.' He was six years old, about six years. So, we had to-- during that time, when I was on the road and I came back, my-- my brother-in-law was gone. They-- they shot-- wherever they found a person, the men, they-- they-- they killed many, many men.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaim what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katransky. So I was in my little town, and then-- then-- then it was-- they made a ghetto in my little town. Of course, there was a-- there were very few people left, very few because most of the men were killed. So we had to do very hard manual work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when you came back, you found that many-- in that two week period--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were missing. Many men were missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many men were missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the ghetto to us. What-- what was it surrounded by?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Routes-- ?] Outside that-- the little town, there was-- there was the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother move?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a section where-- where only Polish people lived there. So we moved there. My mother and I and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her child?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the child, yeah. We were in-- in-- in ghetto, and then, from the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let-- let's just go back for a minute to the ghetto. What was-- what kept all the Jews inside that ghetto? Was there-- was there a fence? Was there a wall? What kept them inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't a fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept people inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could walk. We could walk, only we had to have-- wear patches. It wasn't-- there was not barbed wires or something-- nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? What was your-- what was your living situation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little-- a little room, a small room, and we had a-- we had-- a lady, a person, lived there. A very small room, and we lived there till-- till 1940-- '42, I think. No, from that ghetto, we were sent-- I can't remember exactly. From that ghetto barrack, we were sent-- sent to Kielbasin, that little-- that camp, Kielbasin.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you went there, how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto, I don't know. We had-- Mother use to sell-- people selled-- sold whatever they had. And they went to the peasants, and they-- and they-- they changed it for-- for-- for milk or for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to go through guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Past guards? You were free to go and come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had to sleep there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who guarded it? Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: No one. There were no guards. We were-- we were living there, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your understanding of what the ghetto-- what-- what happened in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: People kept hoping that the world would put an end to the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a new word, 'ghetto'? Was-- was that a new word?

speaker: interviewee
text: To us, it was a new word. I just read in literature about the Jewish ghettos in-- in-- in-- in the Middle Ages, or something in England, or I mean, Spain, or where. To-- to us, it was just a-- again, in that-- we didn't-- ghetto was-- the Germans created that ghetto. They called it a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was nothing keeping you inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no-- nowhere to run, because-- because we couldn't go to the Polish population. We had to go to sleep. Where-- where-- where will we go?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your day? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go to work. The-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds, chop the wood, do all kinds of work, yeah. We worked for the Germans. It was fear. It was constant-- we lived constantly in constant fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come. They used to come to the ghetto, and just shoot, just in-- in the air to frighten the people. Terrorize the people. We were not beaten or something. But it was-- you know, people-- people didn't think of anything. I, myself, didn't think of anything, we just-- it was like a relief from-- for when they got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did little children go to school from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was no school. There was no life. There was no such a life in the ghetto. There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people--

speaker: interviewee
text: People just-- we were dealt-- just-- we were just-- just existing there in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many people do you think lived in there, in the ghetto? How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my little town, it was a very little town. About-- about 80-- 80 families altogether, maybe less.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some of those had already been gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: And more there all Jews in the ghetto? Or were there any other kinds of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no-- there were no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jews were not in-- you know, the ghettos were for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the ghetto for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till about a year, a year and a half, 1940-- 1941 to-- they ended 40-- in '42, it ended in '42. We were sent to the-- to the-- Kielbasin, that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were-- the SS men took us and-- and on trucks. And they brought us to Kielbasin.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who did they-- who did they take to Kielbasin?

speaker: interviewee
text: They deported us from-- to-- to that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The entire ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire ghetto. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: The entire-- everybody went to Kielbasin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who was with you at that time? From your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my-- my mother, no, my sister was in Grodno. I [? don't know how ?] [? she ?] get there. But my mother and me and my brother. My sister-- my brother was also with me Kielbasin. Then he went-- he went to Grodno. Somehow he went-- I don't know he reached Grodno. He was there. Kielbasin, we were two months, till the end of 1942. In 19-- in 1943, I think in January, we were hiding, like-- like I told you, remember? And that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell it for the-- for this story too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding in attic. Don't know if it was in the attic, or it was in [? somewhere. ?] I will say it here in the basement of somewhere. Was it-- we call it [? Melina, ?] hiding place. For three days and three nights, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were you at this time? This was in Kielbasin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grodno, that was in Grodno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you went from Kielbasin to Grodno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to Grodno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was at--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Grodno ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Grodno ghetto. And you joined your sister there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- in Grodno ghetto, I was-- I think I told you, I was working. And then I was-- I was-- they beat me up very badly. And my Bashe told me not to go to work. I had to go-- I had to volunteer to-- to-- in order to get some food-- food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come across Bashe here in Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came from-- she came from Sokolka to Kielbasin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: From other neighboring towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know she was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just met her there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to be with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there in Kielbasin. At-- at-- in Kielbasin, I remember I used to-- we used to-- once, I used to work there. There was a-- a place where there were a lot of potatoes. So I used to-- I used to take the-- once, I remember I-- I-- I stole from that place. And we didn't have any food, nothing to eat. We just-- I don't know how-- how we survived there on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they had some, I think, a kitchen for the people. But it was-- many people died from starvation and from-- from-- became [? very sick. ?] Died out on the street. It was in a way worse than Auschwitz. And-- and then I used-- I used to steal-- they paid us in-- in-- to hide it in my-- I had the-- what do you call it? [INAUDIBLE], in Poland-- in English? [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Under [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came-- I gave to my mother a few potatoes and to Bashe. And-- and we lived in-- in that place. It was-- the camp was full of lice. The lice ate us up there. It was terrible. From Kielbasin, we were sent to Grodno. That's how I came to Grodno, Grodno ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now when-- who was transported from Kielbasin?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the population who came in Kielbasin.

speaker: interviewer
text: The entire population? There was no selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They were-- from all town-- from all the towns, from the neighboring towns. [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], from [INAUDIBLE], I think. They took us all to-- to-- to-- no, the-- I think in groups. Our group went to-- to-- to-- to-- to the ghetto. Maybe some other people went too, maybe straight to the-- to the-- to the concentration camps. I don't-- no, I don't remember that. It's chaos in my-- in my head about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you separated from your family on the trip from Kielbasin to Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I came with my mother. And in Grodno, I met-- we met my brother and my sister. But I lived in a room separately. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with Bashe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was with her mother, I think. But she used to come every day to me, wherever I was. She was almost, whenever she could, she was with me. And then when I-- when I was-- I couldn't-- I was very, very sick, they-- my head was bleeding. And she saw me, she said, you can't go to work. And she said, I'll manage somehow. And-- and I used to lie in my bed. I remember every day, and she used to bring me food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grodno. I never asked her how she got the food. She got it-- she-- I [? think she ?] from her portion. This year, I published a poem. And I simply said that my conscience is very alert about this, about your help. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I survived. If it wasn't for her, I wouldn't have survived [? in there. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then she said-- she said to me-- I think I told her that I don't want you to survive, to [? live ?] after me, because who will take care of you? I remember that. She said to me exactly this conversation was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people die in Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- we were-- I-- when I went-- walked down the streets of Grodno, we saw like a desolate-- everything was desolated. It was like a desert. We didn't have any-- we didn't see any people there. So I don't-- I don't know. We were-- we were like-- it's like-- it was abysmal. I have no words for that-- the ghetto, how it looked, everything. But I remember, we-- we-- we went to that-- we sat on a stone in the ghetto, and we-- we-- we talked. I remember, she talked. I didn't say a word, I remember. I just kept silent. I don't know why. I can't forgive myself now.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she said to me that, I don't-- how can you live without me? Who will take care of you? But later, she changed her mind. There was-- she went to such a place in a synagogue from where they took all the people to the concentration camp. She went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there, was also one lady that we knew that was the-- she was the owner of the house where we were living in Grodno ghetto. So she-- she came-- she give-- and she was freed. They freed her, because her-- her husband was an electrician, I think. She had a privilege to not go to the-- to the-- be deported. She give me a message. It said, Bashe said that I should tell you that you should hide. She's lost already, she said. [INAUDIBLE], in a way. Let-- let-- say-- tell [INAUDIBLE] to hide, to save himself, to hide. I'm lost already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That she wanted me to survive, to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died in-- in-- in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but in-- but in Grodno, she was still--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Grodno ghetto, she was deported before me a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe life in Grodno? You-- you were by your--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no life. We didn't see anything. There was no life, actually. [INAUDIBLE] life to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there work for you after you got better?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- I-- in order to-- people didn't-- everyone didn't go to work. Only they needed-- let's say, every day, they need 200 people to go in someplace for the-- the German authorities told the Judenrat, see to-- to-- to provide them with a list, to give them-- find the people. So people volunteered to go. I used to volunteer to get-- to get food. But then, she didn't let me go-- Bashe. So I didn't go to work. I didn't go to-- after I was beaten up, I didn't go to work. I just was lying in the bed. And she used to come to me every day to bring food and-- and to go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Were there-- there were only Jews in this-- in this ghetto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto is not for-- not for Jews. Whenever you hear about a ghetto, it's for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were-- there were no-- no gypsies or no--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Only in Auschwitz, I-- I-- I-- I-- I heard about gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were others. What about-- who guarded-- who guarded Grodno?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't see-- I didn't see any guards. People lived in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people try to escape that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none that I know of. I read about Grodno, that there were activities. But I-- I didn't see nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any relationship with anybody other than Bashe there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you and Bashe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were selections made from-- from-- from the people in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto, we never had selections. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, [INTERPOSING VOICES] you said she was chosen. She was chosen-- she was selected to leave Grodno before--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, groups. One group left, let's say, in January. Another one left in other months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. But we knew that it was something-- even on the-- on the-- on-- on the road, when we were on the-- [INAUDIBLE] in the-- in the trains. We tried to-- I looked-- we looked for-- for-- for a way to escape. But we couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Bashe was in a different group. What-- who was in her group?

speaker: interviewee
text: She left before me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a few days before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- so who was in her group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. She went-- the other people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to say goodbye to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As I said, that-- that conversation was the goodbye conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the last conversation that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know it would be the last. But that's-- but later, I hide. And a Jewish policeman came to-- he wanted to take me. They looked for people. But I-- I begged him not to let me go, to let me-- let me stay. He let me. He also saved my life. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. He was a classmate of mine in school. There were many people-- quite a few people saved my life. That's how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the-- is that-- when you-- when you hid, is that the experience you referred to earlier? You said you hid for a few days? You were in hiding for a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Grodno. In Grodno. But then, they-- then they kill-- they-- they threw a-- what do you call it? Like it-- it explodes. What do you call it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: A bomb? Oh-- oh, a gas-- I think I--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you say 'granat' in English? When you put something that it's not a bomb, but you-- you-- it explodes it could kill people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. A grenade?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A grenade, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, a hand grenade, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put that where?

speaker: interviewee
text: They threw-- They threw it through the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then took us all to the-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hiding with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, yeah. About 50 people. I also have a poem about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and so you-- you left where-- your hiding place, and you were taken to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Birkenau. How did you-- how did they transport you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought me on the train. Direct from that to-- from Grodno, we went to. And then came--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a train was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I read about later that it was train for animals. We used to-- they used to-- to-- to transport animals there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. A few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any food given?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. No food given.

speaker: interviewer
text: No food. And it was all Jews in the-- in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see people through the windows? Could you see out the windows or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think people-- people used to-- to throw, I think, money or something. But that whatever-- [INAUDIBLE] for the-- for the-- for the-- for the non-Jewish people outside. And they used to give us-- give us water-- to give us water or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the people along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, along the way. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not clear to me, but I remember vaguely all the-- all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you have any information about what was happening in the war? Or-- or where you were going or what Birkenau was like, would be like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew about Auschwitz. We heard about--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear about Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- I don't remember about-- I don't remember of any Auschwitz. There was another place called, [INAUDIBLE], we-- we read about that-- that-- a-- a place, a-- a death camp. We read about this place. We knew it would be-- we would be going to-- to-- to be killed. We knew something that would end. We expected end. People said, you know, said the-- Widduy. You know, Yiddish Widduy that before you die, you confess. There is a Jewish confession called Widduy. So the other people said in-- in the train, I remember, they said this Widduy, this prayer. But we didn't. We just-- [INAUDIBLE] a way to-- to escape. We couldn't. So in Birkenau, we were there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you know where you were? Who announced to you where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't announce. But-- I don't-- no, I can't answer that question. I came there, and that-- that was Birkenau. That was Birkenau, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when you-- when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we came there, [INAUDIBLE] many people, many hundreds, thousands of people talked about Auschwitz-Birkenau. That we called it Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when you arrived in Birkenau? What was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Big, huge houses, barracks. People lived there. There were-- I see nothing except the-- this-- this-- the Auschwitz where the-- the people were-- were-- were living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any selection that went on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I went-- I went to a few selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to-- to-- to work around. Mengele used to stand near us. Naked, we used to work. And they used to see if someone is very, very thin, they used to send to the crematorium. And the first few selections, it was all right. The last selection, I remember, he-- he picked my-- my number. He sent me to the crematory.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sent you to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just the finger. He put--

speaker: interviewer
text: He-- he pointed--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was such a method. If he take the finger to the right, I think, it's to-- to live. To the left-- or the other way around, to work. It was-- he did that, like that. He didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Mengele. We knew it was Mengele. People talked about him. That was Dr. Mengele. He was the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he-- he pointed you in the direction--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, interesting. How did I know? Maybe I didn't know then. Later, after the war. But he was the one who-- see, after the war, I read about Mengele. People talked about him. I don't remember if he was giving his name there, Mengele. I don't remember that. I just know that it was a man who-- who-- who did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was the same man--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the rest of the world, it was-- it only one man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same man for every selection that you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was the man. He was the only man. There was one man that--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the only man who made the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the one. He was the one who made like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any discussion as you were standing in line about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. He just looked at you, and he just made, and you went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was any discussion among the-- among the Jews standing there as to how to pass by him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't discuss. We couldn't even talk. We were standing by prisoners. We were standing in-- in line and everyone was frightened to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were pointed-- they pointed you towards the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I had to go to my barracks. I had to go and to [? stay ?] the night. And in the morning, they used to-- they used to take the trucks and to bring us to the-- to the gas chamber, the crematory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- is that what you were expecting to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to the [? barracks. ?] And I was ready to be-- to be sent for the-- for-- I mean, tomorrow morning to the gas chamber. In the meantime, my brother went to that kapo, his name was Kaminski. Blessed his memory. He was a martyr, even though he was a kapo. It's a misconception about kapos. They're-- they're-- not-- all kapos were not bad. There were a few people who were good people, good kapos, they didn't beat any-- anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that kapo saved my life. Kaminski. Was called Chaim Benesh, they called him-- Kaminski.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was a landsman from my little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Birkenau before this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. About a year, or-- about a year or so. I was altogether about a year and 10 months, and eight-- eight months, or something. So we went to Kaminski. He had-- and he bribed the SS man. He was working in the Sonderkommando. You heard about the Sonderkommando. They used to take the Jews to-- make the Jews ready to go to the-- to the gas chamber. They used to work in the-- near the gas chambers. It was a terrible place to go. We all prayed not to go-- to be sent there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They was to take their own brothers and take-- some recognized their wives, I read about later. And-- they couldn't-- they couldn't-- couldn't do anything. So he saved my life. He talked to the SS men, and they-- they cancelled my name, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother escaped. He said, just came to Chaim Benesh. I talked to him, to Kaminski. He said it was all right. You don't have to go. They didn't call me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people who had also been pointed in that direction?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went. Everyone went. Nothing. I was one of an exception. I was maybe one of all-- of all the others-- the other thousands of the survivors who were sent to the crematories, and didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it like in Birkenau when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an inferno. It was-- it was a hell. People-- the first few months, people died. Hundreds, hundreds of people used to-- some committed-- before I came was even worse. People told me people were-- committed suicide. Men are dead. They-- they used to beat people to death, some people, for-- for little things that they just didn't like. They-- they used to-- to-- to torture them to death. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other people in the barracks with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many. Many non-Jews too. In the barracks, it were-- were not only Jewish. Birkenau was not a camp only for Jewish people. There were many Poles there, and many other Germans, and the homosexuals, and the gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who-- who was with you, specifically? Was there anybody whom you became friendly with? Whom you could talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. We-- we had-- I think I told you about [INAUDIBLE], that man [INAUDIBLE] Erlich. Shayek, Shayek. Maybe I mistake. If I said [INAUDIBLE], it's a mistake. Shayek, his name. Shayek Erlich. He was a good friend of mine. He worked in the underground. Later-- later-- later, I learned that I read about him, that he was one of the leaders of the movement. I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us about your underground--

speaker: interviewee
text: A man came over to me in the-- in the barracks. I think his name was-- it's a one-syllable name-- I think, Munk, or Funk or something, I remember. And he said, you know, we need people to-- to help us in the resistance movement, for the underground movement. And they were doing-- we're helping people, are doing something for-- for-- for the prisoners. Do you want to join us? We-- we heard about you. And we think you'll be-- you'll be-- you'll fit to be a member.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did they hear about you that made them willing [INTERPOSING VOICES] to come and talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They observed me, maybe. I don't know. And I-- I said, yes. And from then, even my own brother didn't know. When I came to Philadelphia, I told my sister-in-law that-- that was a surprise, that-- that-- what I found in the library. I think-- my brother didn't know that, that I was in underground, being in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, when this man came to you, what did-- what-- what did he-- this was Mr. Munk. This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Munk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not quite sure, but it sounded like Munk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a Polish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a Jew-- a Jew from Poland, I think from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old a man do you think he would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- yeah, in his thirties, maybe. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this ends tape number two.
